title: The 8-month-old who startles at every sound
blurb: A previously bright infant who has begun to lose milestones, with an exaggerated startle and a cherry-red spot — and a notably soft, normal-sized belly.

patient.complaint: Losing skills he'd already gained; jumps violently at sudden noises.

opening: 8-month-old boy of Ashkenazi Jewish descent. Parents say he was smiling, rolling, and reaching by 5 months but over the last two months he's gone floppy, stopped tracking, and no longer sits. He has a dramatic startle to sound. Fundoscopy shows a bilateral cherry-red macula. The abdomen is soft with NO hepatosplenomegaly. What do you want to ask, examine, or order next?
history: Term, normal birth, normal newborn screen (this disorder isn't on every panel). Met early milestones, then regressed from ~6 months. Parents are healthy first cousins-once-removed; both of Ashkenazi Jewish ancestry. A maternal great-aunt's baby reportedly died young of a 'brain disease.'
exam: Vitals normal. Hypotonic with poor head control, no longer sits unsupported. Exaggerated startle (hyperacusis) to a hand-clap. Fundoscopy: bilateral cherry-red macula. Increasing head circumference (macrocephaly). Abdomen soft, liver and spleen NOT enlarged. No coarse facies.

prompts.cause: What enzyme is deficient, and what substrate accumulates?
prompts.mechanism: Briefly: why is the spleen/liver NORMAL here (unlike most other sphingolipidoses)?

pinpoint.prompt: Click the sphingolipid-degradation step that fails in Tay-Sachs.
pinpoint.correctMsg: Yes — hexosaminidase A. Without it, GM2 ganglioside can't be degraded and accumulates in neuronal lysosomes (gangliosides are concentrated in the CNS), producing the neurodegeneration and the cherry-red macula. No peripheral organ storage → no hepatosplenomegaly.
pinpoint.wrongMsg: Not quite. The accumulating substrate is GM2 ganglioside — which lysosomal hydrolase normally clears it?
pinpoint.revealMsg: Target: Hexosaminidase A (GM2 ganglioside → Ceramide). Tay-Sachs = Hex A deficiency; Sandhoff = Hex A AND B (β-subunit) with added organomegaly.

mcqs.stem: Why does Tay-Sachs spare the liver and spleen while Niemann-Pick and Gaucher cause hepatosplenomegaly?
choices: GM2 ganglioside is concentrated in neurons/CNS, so storage is mostly neuronal; sphingomyelin and glucocerebroside accumulate in reticuloendothelial (Kupffer/spleen) macrophages
Tay-Sachs patients have protective splenic enzymes
GM2 is water-soluble and excreted by the liver
The liver compensates with hexosaminidase B
mcqs.explanation: Substrate distribution drives the phenotype. Gangliosides are enriched in neuronal membranes, so GM2 storage is CNS-predominant (cherry-red macula, neurodegeneration, NO organomegaly). Sphingomyelin (Niemann-Pick) and glucocerebroside (Gaucher) load reticuloendothelial macrophages → hepatosplenomegaly.

mcqs.stem: The cherry-red macula in Tay-Sachs is produced by:
choices: Ganglioside-laden ganglion cells whiten the perifoveal retina, leaving the thin, well-perfused fovea to stand out red
mcqs.explanation: The retinal ganglion-cell layer is thick around the fovea and stores lipid, turning it pale/white. The fovea itself lacks ganglion cells, so the underlying choroidal red shows through as a 'cherry-red spot.' Also seen in Niemann-Pick and central retinal artery occlusion.

mcqs.stem: How is Sandhoff disease distinguished from classic Tay-Sachs?
choices: Sandhoff lacks BOTH hexosaminidase A and B (β-subunit / HEXB defect) and adds hepatosplenomegaly
mcqs.explanation: Tay-Sachs = HEXA (α-subunit) → only Hex A lost. Sandhoff = HEXB (β-subunit, shared by Hex A and Hex B) → both lost, giving a similar CNS picture PLUS visceral involvement (hepatosplenomegaly). Both show the cherry-red macula.
